title: Boston police seeking suspect in armed robbery
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Boston police are searching for a suspect involved in a Jan. 22 armed robbery in Dorchester.
The robbery occurred around 4:30 p.m. near Adams and Whitten Streets, with multiple victims approached at gunpoint.
Police urge anyone with information to contact detectives at 617-343-4335 or submit anonymous tips through CrimeStoppers.

title: No license yet for World Cup games as Foxborough asks for public safety funding up front
author: Molly Farrar
bullets: Foxborough officials demand $7.8 million in public safety funds up front before issuing a license for 2026 World Cup games.
Boston FIFA World Cup 2026 host committee agrees to cover costs but plans payment after games, risking license denial.
Foxborough requires security funding clarity by March 17; committee seeks to use federal grant with Kraft support.

title: Wu to host first-ever ‘office hours’ for residents to speak with her directly
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Mayor Wu to hold first-ever office hours for residents at Bruce C. Bolling Building in Roxbury.
Event attendees can share city concerns directly and access various city services and registrations.
Over a dozen city departments, including police and housing, will answer questions during the event.

title: 7 players Bruins could target at 2026 NHL trade deadline
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: Bruins consider trading draft picks and prospects for players with term ahead of NHL trade deadline.
Key targets for Bruins include Robert Thomas, Simon Nemec, and Owen Tippett for their potential long-term impact.
Bruins focus on non-rental players to strengthen roster and avoid short-term solutions before trade deadline.

title: Staff member at special needs residential program in Franklin accused of breaking autistic student’s arm
author: Darin Zullo
bullets: Saturs Allison, 27, of Pawtucket, R.I., is accused of breaking an autistic student's arm at a Franklin residential program.
Allison, indicted on assault charges, allegedly caused the injury while managing the student's self-injurious behavior, court records show.
Amego, Inc. employee actions violated restraint policies, and he faces up to five years in prison if convicted, according to filings.

title: ‘Every run is about defying cancer’: This 18-year-old is running Boston for her childhood friend
author: Carson Lyle
bullets: Amanda Lewis, 18, is running the Boston Marathon in honor of her childhood friend who died from brain cancer.
Lewis's participation supports the Dana-Farber Cancer Institute's Marathon Challenge, inspired by her experiences with grief and running.
Her marathon goal is to raise funds for cancer research, continuing her efforts to honor and support cancer fighters and survivors.

title: Fall River man sentenced for raping woman when she refused to take naked pictures of young daughter
author: Carson Lyle
bullets: Elias Rodrigues Dos Santos Mendes, 55, pleaded guilty to rape and child pornography charges in Fall River Superior Court.
Mendes was sentenced to eight to ten years in prison and will be deported to Brazil after serving his sentence.
The offenses included two counts of rape and possession of child pornography; DNA evidence linked Mendes to the crime.

title: What’s next for Patriots’ offense after releasing Stefon Diggs?
author: Conor Ryan
bullets: The Patriots released receiver Stefon Diggs, creating nearly $17 million in cap space for the 2026 NFL offseason.
New England is considering signing AFC free agent Alec Pierce to fill the receiver void left by Diggs' departure.
The Patriots could also explore trading for Eagles' A.J. Brown or Jaguars' Brian Thomas Jr. to bolster their offense.

title: Each mile is for her miracle: This Granby mom is running the Boston Marathon with her daughter in mind
author: Carson Lyle
bullets: Brianna Poehler from Granby, Mass. is running the 2026 Boston Marathon for Boston Children's Hospital's Miles for Miracles.
Poehler's daughter survived a liver transplant at 11 months old, motivating her to participate and raise funds for the hospital.
Her marathon effort honors her daughter's strength and supports research and care for children facing similar medical journeys.

title: Do you support the U.S. strikes on Iran? Here’s what 300 readers said.
author: Autumn Sloboda
bullets: More than 350 readers responded; 54% support U.S. strikes on Iran, 43% oppose, 3% were unsure.
Supporters cite Iran’s regional conflict role and regime criticism; many Iranian readers back the strikes.
Opponents fear broader war, highlight lack of congressional approval, and urge a focus on domestic issues.
